coredata.dc:title: Nitrogen-doped carbonized polymer dots activated by alkalinity of arginine for multicolor multi-platform sensor
coredata.dc:creator: Tang, Yuran
Huang, Chuan
Tang, Huang
Guo, Mingming
coredata.dc:description: Among carbon-based materials, carbon dots (CDs) are popular because of their stable optical properties and good biocompatibility. Their fluorescence properties often are tailored by size, surface modification, or heteroatom doping with multiple precursors. In this paper, the alkalinity of Arginine (Arg) acts as an activator to promote carbonation and increase luminescence efficiency. Meanwhile, Arg acts as a tetrahedron and plays another three roles in preparing amino-modified nitrogen-doped carbonized polymer dots (SA-NCPDs), in which Arg serves as carbon source, nitrogen-doped nitrogen source, and surface modifier. The NCPDs based only on arginine display narrow full width at half maximum (FWHM) of 54 nm. The SA-NCPDs present dual emission distribution in the UV and visible blue regions, respectively. The SA-NCPDs present multicolor emission, especially crimson emission in water under 77 K in different solvents. Besides, SA-NCPDs in different solvents and the prepared polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) composite film have different phosphorescence and long afterglow at 77 K. The excellent biocompatibility and stability of the SA-NCPDs imply it is a potential material in biomedicine. In addition, a multiplatform multicolor ratiometric sensor and visualized colorimetric sensor with high selectivity were constructed successfully for detecting Cu2+, H2O2, and OPD based on self-absorption.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nitrogen-doped carbonized polymer dots
Long afterglow
Multi-platform
Multicolor ratiometric sensor
Multicolor visualized sensor

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdateend pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure e-component body acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst primabst ref specialabst

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Fluorescence emission spectra of A-NCPDs (a), S-NCPDs (b), and SA-NCPDs (c) under different excitation wavelengths; UV absorption spectra of SA-NCPDs and emission spectra under 365 nm excitation (d).

The long afterglow digital pictures of SA-NCPDs dissolved in different solvents under room temperature with sunlight, 365 nm UV light on, and 77 K with 365 nm UV light on and off (up); Digital photo of SA-NCPDs/PVA composite film PVA blank film under room temperature (RT) sunlight and 365 nm UV light on; Long afterglow digital picture of SA-NCPDs/PVA composite film at 77 K.
TEM image (a), size distribution bar graph (b), and HR-TEM image (c) of SA-NCPDs; Illustration of synthesis of SA-NCPDs and its application (d).

Fluorescence emission spectrum (a) and UV-Vis spectrum (b) of SA-NCPDs/OPD/Cu2+/H2O2, SA-NCPDs /Cu2+/H2O2, SA-NCPDs/OPD/Cu2+, SA-NCPDs/OPD/H2O2, Cu2+/H2O2, OPD/H2O2, OPD/Cu2+, SA-NCPDs/OPD, SA-NCPDs/H2O2, SA-NCPDs/Cu2+, SA-NCPDs.

Linear graph of SA-NCPDs/OPD/Cu2+/ H2O2 fluorescence ratio sensor for Cu2+ (a) and fluorescence emission spectrum (b); UV colorimetric digital picture of SA-NCPDs/OPD/Cu2+/ H2O2 sensor for Cu2+(b insert, up); fluorescence colorimetric digital picture of SA-NCPDs/OPD/Cu2+/ H2O2 sensor for Cu2+(b insert, down); linear diagram of SA-NCPDs/OPD/Cu2+/ H2O2 UV absorption colorimetric sensor for Cu2+ (c) and UV–vis curve (d); Error bar: standard deviation (n = 3).

Linearity curve (a) and fluorescence emission spectrum (b) of SA-NCPDs/OPD/Cu2+/H2O2 fluorescence ratio sensor for H2O2; digital picture of SA-NCPDs/OPD/Cu2+/H2O2 UV colorimetric sensor for H2O2 under visible light (c insert, up) and 365 nm UV lamp irradiation (c insert, down); The UV–vis curve (d) of colorimetric sensor for H2O2; Error bar: standard deviation (n = 3).

SA-NCPDs/OPD/ Cu2+/H2O2 fluorescence ratio sensor for OPD linearity curve (a) and fluorescence emission spectrum (b); Anti-interference bar graph of SA-NCPDs/OPD/Cu2+/H2O2 fluorescence ratio sensor (c); Error bar: standard deviation (n = 3).

head.ce:title: Nitrogen-doped carbonized polymer dots activated by alkalinity of arginine for multicolor multi-platform sensor

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Chemical Institute of Chemical Industry, Southwest University, Chongqing 400715, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Chemical Institute of Chemical Industry, Southwest University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Chemical Institute of Chemical Industry, Southwest University, Chongqing 400715, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Among carbon-based materials, carbon dots (CDs) are popular because of their stable optical properties and good biocompatibility. Their fluorescence properties often are tailored by size, surface modification, or heteroatom doping with multiple precursors. In this paper, the alkalinity of Arginine (Arg) acts as an activator to promote carbonation and increase luminescence efficiency. Meanwhile, Arg acts as a tetrahedron and plays another three roles in preparing amino-modified nitrogen-doped carbonized polymer dots (SA-NCPDs), in which Arg serves as carbon source, nitrogen-doped nitrogen source, and surface modifier. The NCPDs based only on arginine display narrow full width at half maximum (FWHM) of 54 nm. The SA-NCPDs present dual emission distribution in the UV and visible blue regions, respectively. The SA-NCPDs present multicolor emission, especially crimson emission in water under 77 K in different solvents. Besides, SA-NCPDs in different solvents and the prepared polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) composite film have different phosphorescence and long afterglow at 77 K. The excellent biocompatibility and stability of the SA-NCPDs imply it is a potential material in biomedicine. In addition, a multiplatform multicolor ratiometric sensor and visualized colorimetric sensor with high selectivity were constructed successfully for detecting Cu2+, H2O2, and OPD based on self-absorption.

ce:section.ce:para: As carbon-based materials, carbon dots (CDs) have long caught the attention of researchers. Because of their excellent photoluminescence properties, chemical stability, environmental protection, and nontoxicity, they became research hotspots in optical, and biomedical applications since they were first discovered [1–4]. One method of preparing CDs from top to bottom is mostly to oxidize, etch or strip bulk solids or powders with sp2 carbon. The fluorescence properties of these CDs are mainly determined by their π-conjugated structure and are easily influenced by the surface or edge [5–7]. But another strategy defined bottom-up method by which small molecules or polymers including plentiful active groups serve as precursors gets more attention [8–10]. Related properties of CDs synthesized were adjusted by controlling the size or crystallographic texture [11], modifying surface groups [9], and doping heteroatoms [12]. Diverse efforts by researchers have been carried out to tune the properties of CDs on individual or dual. For example, strong acid or base acts as an activator during the carbonization. They will help the precursor deprotonation and dehydrogenation, and promote the precursor or polymer chain carbonization to form a CC conjugated structure [13–15]. Ng used bovine serum albumin as a carbon source going through direct carbonization with concentrated strong acid to prepare CDs [16]. Surface passivation can enrich the structure and performance of CDs, making them easier to adjust and control [17,18]. Tang modified the surface of CDs with silver nanoparticles and achieved a highly sensitive surface-enhanced Raman scattering method to selectively detect trace atrazine [19]. Yan modified CDs with 4-aminoantipyrine and utilized them as fluorescent probes for hypochlorite [20]. Heteroatom doping can effectively control the bandgap of CDs and directly affect their fluorescence performance. Nitrogen atom not only dopes into the carbon dots but also adapts the surface and/or edges of CDs, both of which make a huge impact on the luminescence properties of CDs. In particular, carbon source molecules containing primary amines can dope and surface-modify at the same time, playing a dual role [21]. However, it is rare to submit a report for one source working four roles.

Amino acids get a good biocompatibility. At the same time, those small molecules are not only cheap and rich in sources but also abundant in groups such as hydroxyl, carboxyl, and amino groups. They are one of the ideal carbon sources for preparing CDs [22,23]. Lee made β-alanine and L-ascorbic acid carbon sources and nitrogen sources to synthesize nitrogen-doped CDs (NCDs) by microwave method and applied them to cell imaging [24]. Dr. Reisner chose aspartic acid which has a similar structure to citric acid as the carbon source and pyrolyzed it in the air at 320 °C to prepare NCDs [25]. Among amino acids that make up proteins in the human body, arginine (Arg) has the highest isoelectric point of 10.76, which is the strongest alkaline amino acid. At the same time, it owns the highest nitrogen content and important research value in the synthesis of CDs. Some scientific researchers have employed it as a carbon source to prepare unique CDs. In these methods, surface passivators (ethylene diamine or glycerin) are added to modify the surface of CDs to improve their luminescence or structural properties. For instance, Francesca synthesized nitrogen-doped carbon dots with small size and high fluorescence quantum yield using Arg and ethylenediamine as carbon and nitrogen sources [26]. Huang used Arg as a carbon source and glycerin as surface passivator to prepare CDs with high crystallinity and applied them to electrocatalysis [27]. But some surface passivators extra will bring several troubles to the synthesis procedure, and the latter sample purification. Besides, few methods mentioned the influence of the alkalinity of Arg on CDs. And rare study makes Arg a multirole in the synthesis of CDs. Therefore, it is of profound significance to explore the effect of alkalinity and multifunction of Arg on the synthesis of CDs.

The ratio sensor is an ultra-high sensitivity sensor [28]. However, its application has limitations because constructing the sensor requires the luminescent material to have the characteristics of a single excitation and dual emission. Therefore, the design and preparation of a simple and effective ratio sensor based on single excitation and single emission fluorescent materials are worthwhile for increasing application efficiency and expanding the application field of the high-sensitivity sensor.

O-phenylenediamine (OPD) is an intermediate product of industrial materials that can penetrate the human body through the respiratory tract, skin, and gastrointestinal tract to cause toxicity, resulting in methemoglobinemia, liver damage, and hemolytic anemia [29]. Copper (II) (Cu2+) is a heavy metal ion. Moderate content of Cu2+ has a positive effect on the environment and human life, but excessive amounts may burden the body organs, especially the liver and gallbladder, and then lead to metabolic disorders [30]. Hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) plays a major role in food, pharmaceutical, and clinical applications. In biological reactions, H2O2 is a by-product of many enzyme-catalyzed reactions, such as glucose oxidase, and lactate oxidase. So, it is an important signal molecule to detect significant markers such as glucose and lactate in biological metabolic reactions, conducting instant health monitoring for the human body. In addition, the study of oxidative stress-related to tumors, cancer, Parkinson's disease, and aging is usually related to H2O2 [31]. Therefore, it is of great importance to detecting OPD, H2O2, and Cu2+ for environment and human life. A suitable catalyst can catalyze and oxidize OPD to o-benzoquinone (OPDox). OPDox has UV-Vis absorption at 440 nm and emits fluorescence located at 570 nm, which may result in fluorescent radiation self-absorption when the absorbance overlaps with the fluorescence emission of another fluorescence material. Therefore, it has the potential to build ratiometric fluorescence sensor [30].

In this paper, nitrogen-doped carbonized polymer dots (SA-NCPDs) was synthesized by microwave-assisted method with rapid and uniform heating. The carbon skeletons of Arg and sucrose (Suc) acted as ideal carbon sources. Nitrogen-rich arginine was nitrogen source and surface modifier due to its primary amine and imine groups to heteroatom dope and surface modification. Arg increased carbon dots size and acted as an alkaline activator to promote carbon source carbonization. The synthesis mechanism and the role of arginine and sucrose were speculated and revealed according to fluorescence properties, UV–vis absorption, infrared spectrum, and nuclear magnetic resonance spectroscopy (NMR) under different temperature, feed ratio, and reaction time. And the long afterglow of SA-NCPDs in different solvents and SA-NCPDs/polyvinyl alcohol composite film (SA-PVA) under 77 K was remarked. Finally, a multiplatform ratiometric sensor and multicolor visualized sensor to detect Cu2+, H2O2, and OPD was constructed based on SA-NCPDs.

Fig. 1(a) (b) (c) reveal the fluorescence emission spectra of NCPDs only based on Arg (A-NCPDs), NCPDs only based on Suc (S-NCPDs), and SA-NCPDs under different excitation wavelengths. The fluorescence emission spectra of A-NCPDs can be divided into UV emission and visible blue emission. UV emission shows excitation-independence under excitation wavelength (Ex) between 285 nm and 315 nm. But visible blue emission displays excitation dependence at Ex of 325–355 nm. What’s more, the intensity of maximum emission wavelength (Em) in UV emission is tenfold of visible blue emission. The emission spectrum at 360 nm presents a narrow full width at half maximum (about 54 nm). The fluorescence emission spectra of S-NCPDs (Fig. 1b) have obvious excitation dependence, but the intensity of which is very weak with a fluorescence quantum yield (QY) of 0.19% (Table S1). SA-NCPDs are also parted into two emissions, namely, emission 1 at 390 nm and emission 2 lying from 400 nm to 600 nm. Emission 1 has no excitation-dependence, which mainly root in Arg, but plentiful oxygen-containing functional groups from Suc make emission 1 redshift from 360 nm to 390 nm. Emission 2 with obvious excitation-dependence mainly comes from Suc originated from surface state luminescence. Arg makes emission 2 slight blue shift due to its nitrogen doping. And emission intensity raised ten times with a QY of 2.67% (Table S1) since the alkaline environment of Arg promotes Suc deprotonation and the nitrogen doping and amino surface modification promote the increase of fluorescence efficiency. The maximum Ex of 365 nm and Em of 450 nm are easy to gain.

From the UV absorption spectrum in Fig. 1d, it’s effortless to find the UV absorption of SA-NCPDs range between 280–600 nm and has two UV absorption peaks. Absorption 1 is sharp and thin, centered at about 300 nm, which originates from the π-π * transition of the CC of SA-NCPDs, evidencing the origin of UV emission of A-NCPDs and emission 1 of SA-NCPDs. The other is a weak peak at 340 nm, which originates from the n-π * transition caused by heteroatom doping. The results point outs the SA-NCPDs have two emission centers, confirmed by the time-resolved fluorescence spectrum (Fig. S1, Table S1).

The reaction mechanism was found out by changing the feed ratio of Arg and Suc (Table S2). As sketched in the three-dimensional fluorescence spectra ( Fig. 2), SAn3-SAn9 showed two emissions of distinct degrees in UV and blue regions, named emission (a) and emission (b). As the ratio of Arg decreased, emission (a) at 390 nm gradually weakened, which was in agreement with the previous speculation, meaning that emission (a) came from Arg. In addition, with the decrease of Arg, the intensity of Emission (b) increased and then decreased, implying that moderate Arg contributed to the increase of fluorescence intensity of NCPDs. Also, pH in TableS3 stands for the pH of the solution after reaction (pHa) reducing with the reduction of Arg from SAn3 to SAn5. But because pHa is still alkaline, the fluorescence intensity of NCPDs still raises. When the ratio of Arg falls to a certain level, the pHa turns neutral or acidic, such as SAn6-SAn9. With the decrease of arginine, the pHa decreases sharply (Fig. S2 c), and so does the fluorescence intensity. This represents alkaline conditions are key to regulate the fluorescence intensity of NCPDs.

To further explore the influence of the ratio of Arg to Suc on its optical properties, we collected the UV-Vis absorbance spectra of the reaction solution as displayed in Fig. S2 a. The UV-Vis absorption range of SAn1 is 280–380 nm. And SAn1 has two UV absorption peaks at 300 nm and 340 nm, which are derived from the π-π * transition of the carbon-carbon double bond and the n-π * transition caused by heteroatomic doping in SAn1, respectively. The UV-Vis absorption spectrum stands for the A-NCPDs has both carbon core and the surface containing rich functional groups, which are consistent with the fluorescence spectrum. Compared with SAn1, SAn2 has a longer UV absorption line (280–600 nm), and a sharp and narrow maximum absorption peak (308 nm), corresponding to the π-π * transition of the carbon-carbon double bond. When the two compounds reacted with different ratios, the UV absorption of SA-NCPDs has the same characteristics as that of the two compounds, which has a sharp and narrow absorption peak at 300 nm, a wide absorption peak at 340 nm, and the absorption range extending to 600 nm. The UV-Vis absorption spectrum revealed that Arg and Suc had a significant effect on the final NCPDs structure, which was in line with our design idea.

To explore the influence on the structure of the final product, FTIR characterization was carried out on the product, and the results are exhibited in Fig. S2 b. The FTIR spectrum of SAn3-SAn9 shows the surface of NCPDs contains OH/NH (3227 cm−1), C-H (2930 cm−1, 2873 cm−1), CO (1765 cm−1, 1702 cm−1), CC (1590 cm−1), CN (1556 cm−1), C-N (1481 cm−1,1451 cm−1), C-O (1145 cm−1), and C-O-C (1350 cm−1, 1040 cm−1). As Arg reduces, the peak of -OH increases and widens. This is stem from the fact the intermolecular hydrogen bond becomes stronger as the pH of the solution decreases after the reaction. -C-O-C- (1040 cm−1 and 1350 cm−1) gradually increased, hinting that surface oxygen-containing functional groups increased with the excess of Suc. The C-O (1145 cm−1) stretching vibration of alcohol began to occur at SAn7 as Suc remained excessive. These results suggest the structure of NCPDs is regulated by Arg and Suc.
In summary, preliminary speculation combined with literature and characterization is present.

In the beginning, Arg and Suc decomposed in high temperature, high-pressure, and the basic condition of Arg and undergo maillard reaction. And then the products undergo an amidation reaction and hydrothermal cross-linking polymerization. As the reaction time and temperature increase, the polymerized product undergoes further entangling and cross-linking. The cross-linking center is gradually carbonized to form a carbon nucleus. In this process, Arg not only acted as a nitrogen source to dope the carbon nucleus with nitrogen to raise its fluorescence efficiency. At the same time, its surface modification functional groups adjust the surface of the product so its surface is full of amino functional groups [32].

Self-quenching existed in different concentrations of SA-NCPDs (Fig. S3, S4 a). It can be observed that as the concentration increases, the fluorescence spectrum of SA-NCPDs gradually redshifts, and the fluorescence intensity first increases and then decreases. This may be explained by the internal filtration effect of high concentration of the solution or the interaction between SA-NCPDs at a high concentration to form dimers.

Solvents often have a potent effect on fluorescence properties. To explore the influence of solvents on the fluorescence properties of SA-NCPDs, SA-NCPDs were dissolved in different solvents, and fluorescence spectra were collected (Fig. S4 b). The fluorescence spectrum of SA-NCPDs red-shifts only in protic solvents or alcohol solvents. This may be thanks to forming hydrogen bonds or protonation between SA-NCPDs and solvents. At low temperatures, SA-NCPDs dissolved in different solvents emit different fluorescence. Besides, SA-NCPDs in different solvents have a duration of 8 s long afterglow with the naked eye after UV light off, as displayed in Fig. 3 and Supplementary video 1. Among these, SA-NCPDs in aqueous solution emit crimson fluorescence, and DMF\DMSO\glycerol emit light yellow fluorescence.
Supplementary material related to this article can be found online at doi:10.1016/j.colsurfb.2022.112517.

The following is the Supplementary material related to this article Video S2.
Video S1
.

To explore the influence of acidity and alkalinity on the fluorescence performance of SA-NCPDs, the same concentration of SA-NCPDs solutions was prepared with solutions of different pH. Fluorescence emission spectra under specific excitation were measured (Fig. S4 c). While the fluorescence intensity of SA-NCPDs is stable in the pH range of 4–11, the fluorescence intensity decreases sharply at strong acid (pH=1–3) or strong base (pH=12–13) condition.

To explore the photostability of SA-NCPDs, the SA-NCPDs were lighted with 365 nm UV light for about 3600 s, and the fluorescence spectra were collected at different time, as shown in Fig. S5 a. After 1 h lighting, the fluorescence intensity of SA-NCPDs remained above 85%, representing it has good photobleaching properties. To explore anti-ionic toxicity of SA-NCPDs, different concentrations of NaCl were added to SA-NCPDs, and then fluorescence spectra were collected, as displayed in Fig. S5 b. As the concentration of NaCl increases, the fluorescence intensity decreases slowly. When the NaCl concentration is 2.5 M, the fluorescence intensity can still maintain at 90%, meaning the SA-NCPDs have good anti-ionic toxicity.

Based on the photostability of the SA-NCPDs, the SA-NCPDs/PVA composite film was prepared, as presented in Fig. 3. Under the natural light at room temperature, the blank PVA film was uniform and transparent, while the SA-CDs/PVA film was uniform and transparent but yellow due to introduction of CDs. Under a 365 nm UV lamp irradiating, the SA-CDs/PVA film emits blue fluorescence, while the blank PVA film has no fluorescence. This shows the CDs have application potential in agricultural film. In addition, the film has a low-temperature long afterglow visible to the naked eye for about 2 s at 77 K, as proved in Fig. 3 and Supplementary video 2.
Supplementary material related to this article can be found online at doi:10.1016/j.colsurfb.2022.112517.

The following is the Supplementary material related to this article Video S3.
Video S2
.

To study the biocompatibility of SA-NCPDs, Hela cells serve as the research object to explore the cytotoxicity of SA-NCPDs at different concentrations with the standard MTT method. The cell viability of 500 μg/mL or 1000 μg/mL SA-NCPDs maintained at 87% or 75% after 24 h of cell culture (Fig. S6). The results show the SA-NCPDs had low cytotoxicity and good biocompatibility.

The NCPDs synthesized by equimolar ratio of Arg and Suc and reacted at 210 °C for 10 min are called SA-NCPDs, detailed procedure was described in Supplementary Information S1. TEM in Fig. 4 reveals the average diameter of SA-NCPDs is 5.51 nm which distribute between 3.5 and 7.5 nm. The HRTEM image makes it clear the SA-NCPDs have a lattice spacing of 0.21 nm which is corresponding to the (100) plane of graphite. Further, the TEM image (Fig. S7 a, b) of S-NCPDs displays that S-NCPDs had an average diameter of 2.34 nm, meaning adding Arg increases the size of SA-NCPDs. The HRTEM image in Fig. S7 c shows that S-NCPDs have an identical lattice spacing to SA-NCPDs.

To explore the chemical composition of SA-NCPDs, XPS and FTIR were performed on the surface of SA-NCPDs, as shown in Fig. S8, and Fig. S2 (b SAn6). It can be seen from the full spectrum of XPS the surface of SA-NCPDs mainly contains C (284.70 eV, C1s, 66.25%), N (400.97 eV, N1s, 13.57%), O (531.32 eV, O1s, 20.18%) (Fig. S8). O1s are mainly split into two categories, namely 532.37 eV (C-O) and 531.32 eV (CO). The N1s are mainly divided into three categories, namely 400.97 eV (pyrrolic N), 398.61 eV (Pyrisinie N), and 399.77 eV (Amine N). The C1s are mainly divided into three categories, namely 284.7 eV (C-C/CC), 287.98 eV CO/CN), 285 eV (C-N/ C-O). This result means that Arg successfully carried out nitrogen doping and amino surface modification on SA-NCPDs.

The 1H NMR spectrum of Arg + Suc mixed solution, A-NCPDs, S-NCPDs, and SA-NCPDs (Fig. S9) stand for that S-NCPD and SA-NCPDs contain aldehyde hydrogens, unsaturated hydrogens, hydrogen connected to heteroatoms, and saturated hydrogen, which is consistent with the results of XPS and FTIR. It insinuates that Suc and Arg undergo a series of decomposition during the reaction, dehydration, or decarboxylation to form an aldehyde group and an unsaturated carbon structure.

As shown in Fig. 5a, comparing different sensors, SA+Cu2++H2O2, Cu2++H2O2, SA+H2O2, SA+Cu2+, and SA only have the emission peak at 450 nm when there is no OPD. Only SA, H2O2 and OPD coexist but no Cu2+, there is only an emission peak at 450 nm. Once Cu2+ and OPD coexist, the fluorescence peak at 570 nm appears, namely, SA+Cu2++OPD, Cu2++OPD, SA+Cu2++H2O2 +OPD. And when SA-NCPDs, OPD, Cu2+, and the H2O2 coexist, the fluorescence intensity of OPDox at 570 nm reaches the maximum, and the fluorescence intensity of SA-NCPDs at 450 nm is the smallest. At the same time, the UV absorption spectrum (Fig. 5b) also confirmed this point. This phenomenon may be explained by the fluorescence resonance energy transfer or the internal filtering effect, which makes the fluorescence of SA-NCPDs weaken and the fluorescence intensity of OPDox increases.

To improve the sensitivity of the sensor, we optimize the incubation time, concentration of SA-NCPDs, incubation temperature, pH, and concentration of OPD of the sensor. The optimization results are posted on Fig. S10 (a, b, c, d, e) and analyzed in S3 [33,34].

To sum up, the ratio of the intensity of emission at 570 nm (F570) to the intensity of emission at 450 nm (F450), named F570/F450, was calculated to study the linear response of the sensor for detecting Cu2+. Cu2+ plays as an oxidizing agent to oxidize OPD to OPDox. Therefore, a yield of OPDox was determined by the concentration of Cu2+ to some extent. Fig. 6(c) and (d) present the prepared multicolor visualized sensor has good linearity with Cu2+ in the range of 1–40 μM and 40–100 μM. The linear equations are A= 0.0327c+ 0.134 (R2 =0.947), A= 0.00757c+ 1.09(R2 =0.980) with a limit of detection (LOD) of 0.3 μM (S/N = 3). The color of the solution gradually changed from colorless and transparent, to light yellow, then yellow, and further kelly as the copper ion concentration increased under visible light (Fig. 6b insert, upon). Meanwhile, the multicolor ratiometric sensor has good linearity with Cu2+in the range of 1–8 μM and 8–80 μM, and the linear equations are F= 0.156c+ 0.297 (R2 =0.977), F= 0.345c-1.08 (R2 =0.976) with LOD of 0.1 μM (S/N = 3) (Fig. 6a, b). As the concentration of copper ions increases, the solution gradually changes from dark blue to light blue, then shallow orange, orange, and even yellow emission under 365 nm UV lamp irradiation (Fig. 6b insert, down). The results prove the multicolor ratiometric fluorescent sensor and visualized colorimetric sensor for Cu2+ have been successfully constructed.

To test the anti-interference performance of the sensor for Cu2+, Cu2+ was replaced with Fe3+, Fe2+, K+, Zn2+, Ag+, Ni2+, Co2+. At the same time, copper salt and other metal salt solutions containing different anions are used to explore the influence of anions on the sensor. As shown in Fig. 8c, only when Cu2+ exists, the F570/F450 value is the largest certifying the sensor has good selectivity to Cu2+.

In addition, the F570/F450 and UV-Vis spectrum of the sensor to detect H2O2 were presented in Fig. 7·H2O2 acts as a catalyst to hasten OPD oxidation. Within a certain amount of H2O2, a yield of OPDox was agreed upon by the amount of H2O2. Fig. 7a, b showed the multicolor ratiometric fluorescence sensor has good linearity with H2O2 in the range of 0.05–5 mM. The linear equations are F= 0.531c+ 0.850 (R2 =0.952) with LOD of 0.03 mM (S/N = 3). As the concentration of H2O2 increases, the solution gradually changes from dark blue to light blue, then faint yellow, and then yellow emission under 365 nm UV lamp irradiation (Fig. 7c insert, down). The color of the solution gradually changes from light yellow to dark yellow as the concentration of H2O2 increases under visible light (Fig. 7b insert, upon), proving a multicolor ratiometric fluorescent sensor and visualized colorimetric sensor for detecting H2O2 have been successfully constructed.

Furthermore, with a certain amount of catalyst and oxidant, OPD as a reactant is positively correlated with the yield of OPDox. Therefore, the ratiometric fluorescence sensor has good linearity with OPD in the range of 0.1–0.5 M ( Fig. 8a, b). The linear equations are F= 0.423c+ 0.210 with LOD of 0.02 M (S/N = 3), certifying that a ratiometric fluorescence sensor for detecting OPD has been successfully constructed.

In this paper, strong alkalinity and high nitrogen content of Arg were implemented to promote itself and Suc carbonization, nitrogen doping, and surface modification. SA-NCPDs with abundant nitrogen and oxygen functional groups on the surface were prepared by high temperature and high-pressure conditions. Combining the structural characterization of the product, fluorescence performance, UV-Vis absorption, infrared spectroscopy, it is inferred the raw materials undergo hydrothermal cross-linking polymerization through decomposition, dehydration and decarboxylation, amidation reactions, and then carbonization. The prepared SA-NCPDs have different emission long time low-temperature phosphorescence in different solvents and after being prepared into composite film. The cytotoxicity experiment proved that SA-NCPDs have excellent biocompatibility and are appropriate for the biomedical field. Finally, the SA-NCPDs were used to construct multiplatform multicolor fluorescence ratiometric sensors and visualized colorimetric sensors to detect Cu2+, H2O2, and OPD, which expand the field of material selection for ratiometric sensors.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities: Southwest University (NO. 5330500110, NO.5240101332), China, Mingming Guo. National Training Program of Innovation and Entrepreneurship for Undergraduates (NO. 202110635101), China, Huang Tang. Provincial Training Program of Innovation and Entrepreneurship for Undergraduates (NO.7110600001/184), China, Mingming Guo.

ce:section.ce:para: Yuran Tang: Conceptualization, Validation, Methodology, Formal analysis, Writing. Chuan Huang: Resources, Validation. Huang Tang: Funding acquisition, Supervision. Mingming Guo: Resources, Methodology, Funding acquisition, Supervision.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.colsurfb.2022.112517.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
Supplementary material
.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Red, yellow, and blue luminescence by graphene quantum dots: syntheses, mechanism, and cellular imaging

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Gao, T. Wang, X. Yang, L. et al. Red, yellow, and blue luminescence by graphene quantum dots: syntheses, mechanism, and cellular imaging. ACS Appl Mater Interfaces, 2017, 9 (29), 24846–24856
Baker, S.N. Baker, G.A. Luminescent carbon nanodots: emergent nanolights. Angew Chem Int Ed Engl, 2010, 49 (38), 6726–6744.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Jiang, L. Ding, H. Lu, S. et al. Photoactivated fluorescence enhancement in F, N-Doped carbon dots with piezochromic behavior. Angew Chem Int Ed Engl, 2019, 59 (25), 9986–9991.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Hu, C. Li, M. Qiu, J. et al. Design and fabrication of carbon dots for energy conversion and storage. Chem Soc Rev, 2019, 48 (8), 2315–2337.
